A traveling merchant, passing through last week.
My cousin, who lives near the border.
One of the town guards. He seemed shaken when he told me.
An old friend who works the northern farms.
A peddler who comes through every few months.
The innkeeper. She hears everything sooner or later.
A hunter who was out in the woods past dark.
Someone at the market. Couldn't tell you who exactly.
A cartwright who makes deliveries to the outer settlements.
My sister, who has a knack for finding things out.
A woodcutter from the logging camp up the ridge.
The herbalist. She doesn't gossip lightly, so I paid attention.
A courier who rides the south road every fortnight.
One of the fisherfolk down by the river.
A shepherd who grazes his flock near the old ruins.
A mason who was doing repair work on the wall.
A trapper who works the marshland to the east.
My neighbor's eldest, who helps with the supply runs.
A traveling tinker who fixes pots and sharpens blades.
The well-digger. He goes everywhere and talks to everyone.
A forester who patrols the tree line at dawn.
One of the road wardens from the crossroads post.
My uncle, who keeps bees near the northern meadow.
A charcoal burner who lives alone in the woods.
The ferry operator across the river. Patient man, good memory.
A mule driver who hauls goods between settlements.
One of the midwives. She visits every household eventually.
A stonecutter working the quarry on the west slope.
The tanner's apprentice, who came from another village originally.
An old woman who sells dried herbs at the roadside.
A soldier passing through on reassignment. He barely said a word, but what he said stuck.
One of the grain haulers. They talk to each other on the long road.
A brewer from two valleys over who trades with our innkeeper.
The farrier, who shoes horses for every rider who passes through.
My brother-in-law, who works the docks in the port town.
A traveling scribe who records local histories.
The chandler's wife. She knows more than most give her credit for.
A seasonal laborer who comes every harvest and leaves after.
One of the children, actually. Overheard something they shouldn't have.
The blacksmith's journeyman, who arrived from the capital last month.
A beekeeper who trades honey at the monthly market.
An elderly neighbor who hasn't left her house in years but somehow knows everything.
A river barge pilot who stops here to resupply.
The man who cleans the temple. You'd be surprised what people say when they think no one's listening.
A goatherd from the high pastures who only comes down when the weather turns.
One of the watchtower sentries. He's up there all night with nothing to do but watch and think.
A wool trader who visits three or four times a year.
The miller. Everyone passes through the mill eventually.
A woman who runs the supply cache at the halfway shelter on the east road.
My mother, who heard it from someone at the shrine.
A bridge keeper two crossings downstream.
The gravedigger. Odd source, I know, but he was certain.
A potter who travels between settlements selling wares from a handcart.
One of the surveyors marking out the new boundary lines.
A farmhand who sleeps in the barn and claims to have seen it with his own eyes.
The dyer's daughter, who runs errands to the neighboring villages.
A retired soldier who settled here for the quiet. So much for quiet.
A lamp lighter from the next town over who crossed paths with our courier.
My apprentice, who picked it up from the other apprentices. They talk more than they work.
A cloth merchant from the southern road. She was reluctant to say much.
The night watchman. He hears things in the dark that daylight folk don't.
A healer who was called to the outer farms last week.
One of the fence builders working the perimeter. He overhears the guards talking.
A rat catcher who works the cellars and tunnels under the older buildings.
My father, who heard it at the council meeting he wasn't supposed to attend.
A wandering musician who plays at taverns along the trade road.
The cheese maker, who gets her milk from farms spread across the whole valley.
A thatcher who was up on a roof and overheard a conversation below.
One of the teamsters who moves freight for the trade consortium.
A falconer who ranges wide and comes back with more than birds.
The widow at the end of the lane. She sits by her window all day and sees everything.
A horse trader who deals with every settlement within three days' ride.
My daughter. She came home from the market with it. I wish she hadn't.
One of the smelters from the ore works upstream.
A mapmaker passing through to update the regional charts.
The settlement's bookkeeper. Numbers tell stories if you know how to read them.
A basket weaver who trades at every market from here to the coast.
One of the orchard workers, during the last pruning season.
A tanner from the next settlement, visiting to buy hides.
The woman who runs the communal bakehouse. People talk while they wait for bread.
A prospector who wanders the hills looking for ore and finding gossip instead.
One of the boat builders down at the landing. He heard it from a fisherman.
A pilgrim, of all people. Stopped for water and left a story in exchange.
The stable hand. Travelers tell their horses things they wouldn't tell a person. The stable hand listens.
My grandfather, who hasn't been wrong about these things yet.
A border patrol rider who stopped for a meal on her way south.
The soap maker, who visits every household to collect tallow.
A furrier who traps along the northern ridge and trades at the seasonal market.
One of the dam workers maintaining the reservoir upstream.
A glazier who was called in to repair a window and stayed for the gossip.
My eldest son. He works the supply route and hears things between stops.
The weaver, who heard it from a flax farmer who heard it from a miller who heard it from a guard.
A salt merchant from the lowlands. She spoke carefully, like she was choosing every word.
One of the stone haulers building the new foundation. They work alongside laborers from everywhere.
A birdwatcher, believe it or not. Spends all day in the hills with nothing but time and sharp eyes.
The elder's personal attendant. He didn't mean to say it. It slipped.
A cobbler who repairs boots for the road wardens.
One of the seasonal herders who moves livestock through our territory.
A well-sinker who was testing the aquifer south of the settlement.
The cooper. He makes barrels for everyone, which means he talks to everyone.
My wife's aunt, who lives three days east and sends letters by every courier.
A bone setter who was treating an injury at the logging camp.
One of the hive tenders working the community apiaries.
A candle maker who sources wax from settlements across the region.
The tax collector's clerk. He writes everything down, including things he shouldn't.
A net mender from the riverside district. She hears the fishermen talk.
My old commanding officer, who stopped through on his way to retirement.
A truffle hunter and his dog. The dog found the truffles. The man found the rumor.
One of the smoke-house workers. People forget they're there, and talk freely.
A wheelwright from the capital who came to inspect the road conditions.
The woman who manages the seed exchange. Farmers tell her everything when they're worried about next season.
A night fisher who works the river under moonlight. He saw something.
My neighbor. We share a wall. Walls are thinner than people think.
A rope maker who supplies every farm and fishing crew in the valley.
One of the pavers working the main road. He hears every conversation that passes by.
A beeswax trader who collects from hives scattered across three territories.
The woman who washes linens for the inn. She's seen and heard more than anyone.
A fence post surveyor who walks the entire perimeter once a season.
Someone reliable. I promised I wouldn't say who.
A leather worker who stitches saddles for the guard post.
One of the tile makers from the clayworks on the eastern bank.
A vintner from the slopes who brings last year's pressing to market.
The settlement historian, who rarely speaks but chose to speak about this.
A traveling dentist who pulls teeth and gathers stories in equal measure.
My youngest, who overheard the guards talking when she was supposed to be in bed.
One of the reed cutters from the wetlands. Quiet folk, but sharp ears.
A grave tender who maintains the old plots on the hill. Says he's seen things.
The dogcatcher. He wanders every corner of this settlement. Hears everything.
A loom worker from the textile shed, during the night shift.
One of the dairy hands from the eastern farm cooperative.
A goose girl, of all people. She takes them to pasture and comes back with news.
My old teacher. She never says anything without thinking it through first.
A lamplighter who works the main road between here and the crossroads.
The privy cleaner. Nobody talks to him, so people talk near him like he isn't there.
A barrel maker's widow who still runs the workshop. She supplies half the region.
One of the scouts who maps game trails through the backcountry.
A newcomer who just settled here. Said it's why they left where they came from.
